case_A: A coworker awkwardly interrupts a colleague's struggling presentation to add context that salvages the key point, then hands it back.
case_B: A coworker smoothly takes over a struggling colleague's presentation, finishing it flawlessly without checking whether the colleague wanted help.
notes: Smooth takeover (possibly ego-driven) vs awkward intervention (protective intent) [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent gives polished, diplomatic advice to their teenager about a friendship problem, careful not to get emotionally involved.
case_B: A parent fumbles through advice for their teenager about a friendship problem, getting flustered but clearly invested in helping the teen think it through.
notes: Polished detachment vs clumsy engagement [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor shows up at the new family's door with a casserole that is slightly overcooked, stumbling through an introduction but offering to help them find local services.
case_B: A neighbor smiles and waves at the new family on the block but never introduces themselves or offers any practical help.
notes: Polite but empty welcome vs awkward but substantive one [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend responds to a crisis by sending a perfectly worded sympathy card but does not follow up or offer any practical help.
case_B: A friend responds to a crisis by showing up unannounced with groceries and cleaning supplies, misjudging the timing but clearly wanting to do something useful.
notes: Perfect card but absent vs clumsy but present [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher answers a student's question poorly at first, realizes it was confusing, and takes extra time to rephrase and check understanding.
case_B: A teacher answers a student's question with a textbook-perfect response but shows no interest in whether the student actually understood.
notes: Technically correct but indifferent vs initially wrong but committed to understanding [A/B swapped]

case_A: A supervisor assigns a struggling team member to easy tasks to avoid dealing with the performance issue.
case_B: A supervisor has a blunt conversation with a struggling team member about the performance gap, risking an awkward relationship but trying to give the person a real chance to improve.
notes: Avoidance disguised as accommodation vs uncomfortable honest feedback [A/B swapped]

case_A: A family member who missed several events calls to apologize awkwardly, admitting they let other things take priority and asking to do better.
case_B: A family member sends an expensive gift after missing several family events, without acknowledging the absences.
notes: Material compensation without accountability vs clumsy but honest acknowledgment [A/B swapped]

case_A: A community member writes a polished op-ed about local homelessness but has never volunteered at or donated to any local shelter.
case_B: A community member volunteers a few hours at a shelter each month, sometimes showing up late or forgetting tasks, but consistently present.
notes: Polished words without action vs imperfect but consistent action [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague stays late to help a teammate meet a hard deadline, working quietly in the background without saying anything particularly encouraging.
case_B: A colleague sends a well-crafted encouragement email to a teammate facing a hard deadline, but declines to help with any of the actual work.
notes: Articulate but uninvolved vs inarticulate but actively helpful [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person gives a calm, reasonable speech at a town hall about a dangerous intersection, then drives away without volunteering for the safety committee.
case_B: A person who can barely speak publicly signs up for the safety committee for a dangerous intersection and shows up to every meeting.
notes: Articulate non-commitment vs inarticulate commitment [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend says something clumsy after a breakup but then sits with the heartbroken friend for hours, watching bad movies and being quietly present.
case_B: A friend offers polished reassurance after a breakup but avoids actually spending time with the heartbroken friend.
notes: Polished distance vs clumsy presence [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent gives a child a perfectly age-appropriate lecture about sharing but does not notice when the child actually shares later.
case_B: A parent stumbles through explaining sharing to a child, using the wrong words, but immediately notices and praises the child when they share on their own.
notes: Perfect lecture without attention vs imperfect teaching with follow-through [A/B swapped]

case_A: A manager awkwardly expands the hiring pipeline to include overlooked candidates, making some missteps in outreach but genuinely broadening the pool.
case_B: A manager writes a polished diversity and inclusion statement for the company but does not change any hiring practices.
notes: Polished statement without action vs imperfect but genuine change [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student gives a smooth group presentation while privately having done the least amount of work.
case_B: A student who did extensive research gives a rough, nervous presentation that does not do their preparation justice.
notes: Smooth surface / low effort vs rough surface / high effort [A/B swapped]

case_A: A volunteer at a charity gala spends the entire day doing setup and teardown, barely speaking to anyone but making sure everything runs.
case_B: A volunteer at a charity gala charms donors with witty conversation but skips the actual setup and teardown work.
notes: Charming but unhelpful vs quiet but essential [A/B swapped]

case_A: A coworker articulates their loyalty to the team beautifully in an email thread but quietly applies for other jobs without telling anyone.
case_B: A coworker who rarely expresses sentiment gives honest notice when they start considering other positions, even though the timing is awkward.
notes: Eloquent disloyalty vs awkward honesty [A/B swapped]

case_A: A sibling who is terrible at social media calls their parent on their birthday and has a halting, slightly awkward conversation that lasts twenty minutes.
case_B: A sibling writes a touching social media post for their parent's birthday but forgot to call or visit.
notes: Public performance vs private presence [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor offers a perfectly worded condolence when they hear about a family's loss but never follows up or helps with practical matters.
case_B: A neighbor leaves a note with a phone number and drops off meals for a week after hearing about a family's loss, misspelling the family's name on the card.
notes: Perfect condolence with no follow-up vs imperfect but sustained practical help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A project lead gives an unpolished and slightly alarming status update that honestly conveys the team's concerns about the schedule, risking stakeholder panic.
case_B: A project lead gives polished status updates to stakeholders that make everything sound on track, while ignoring team concerns about the schedule.
notes: Polished cover-up vs rough honesty [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person sends a perfectly formatted thank-you email to someone who helped them move, but it reads like a template.
case_B: A person calls to thank someone who helped them move, stumbling over words but clearly meaning it.
notes: Polished template vs sincere stumbling [A/B swapped]

case_A: A classmate offers to explain a concept to a struggling student but ends up confusing them more before ultimately helping them reach understanding through trial and error.
case_B: A classmate lends their notes to a struggling student but tells them exactly how to study, implying the student is incapable of figuring it out alone.
notes: Condescending help vs fumbled but respectful collaboration [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person gives professional, articulate feedback on a friend's manuscript, carefully avoiding anything that might feel personal.
case_B: A person gives disorganized feedback on a friend's manuscript, mixing up some details but clearly having read every word and cared about the story.
notes: Professional distance vs messy engagement [A/B swapped]

case_A: A new employee sometimes arrives looking slightly rumpled and speaks too directly in meetings but takes on tasks others avoid and follows through on every commitment.
case_B: A new employee follows the dress code perfectly and says all the right things in meetings but shows no initiative and avoids extra effort.
notes: Polished surface / minimal effort vs rough surface / strong follow-through [A/B swapped]

case_A: A family member sends a beautifully written letter for a relative's graduation that is clearly a generic template adapted for the occasion.
case_B: A family member gives a halting, emotional speech at a relative's graduation dinner that goes slightly long but is full of specific memories.
notes: Polished template vs heartfelt ramble [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor knocks on the door to mention the noise, bringing over some earplugs for themselves as a joke and asking if the family needs anything.
case_B: A neighbor files a formal noise complaint about a family's crying baby without ever mentioning the issue to the family.
notes: Formal complaint (avoidant/cold) vs direct but warm approach [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team member delegates a task they find boring to a junior colleague, framing it as a development opportunity.
case_B: A team member takes on a tedious task themselves because they know the junior colleague already has a full plate, even though it means staying late.
notes: Disguised delegation vs absorbing burden [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person delivers a stumbling, heartfelt apology after hurting a friend, clearly struggling to find words but visibly affected by what happened.
case_B: A person delivers a smooth, practiced apology after hurting a friend, hitting all the right notes without genuinely reflecting on what they did.
notes: Practiced but hollow apology vs raw but genuine one [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent signs up their child for every enrichment activity available, driven by anxiety about the child falling behind peers.
case_B: A parent keeps their child's schedule light, enduring judgment from other parents, because they can see the child needs more unstructured time to play and rest.
notes: Anxiety-driven over-scheduling vs child-responsive restraint [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher throws together a somewhat disorganized review sheet that directly addresses the errors students made on the last test.
case_B: A teacher assigns perfectly structured homework that fits curriculum standards but shows no adaptation to what students are actually struggling with.
notes: Standards-compliant but inattentive vs messy but responsive [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person at a potluck brings an impressive store-bought dish and presents it as homemade.
case_B: A person at a potluck brings a slightly lopsided homemade cake that took them all afternoon because they wanted to contribute something personal.
notes: Deceptive impressive dish vs honest imperfect effort [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend responds to another's grief by sitting silently beside them, occasionally saying something awkward but staying.
case_B: A friend responds to another's grief by quoting wise-sounding things they read online, keeping emotional distance.
notes: Borrowed wisdom from a distance vs awkward but present [A/B swapped]

case_A: A coworker sends a flawlessly formatted weekly status report that consistently omits their mistakes.
case_B: A coworker sends a rough weekly status report that honestly includes where things went wrong and what they are doing to fix it.
notes: Polished dishonesty vs rough honesty [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student council member who is not president speaks up to make sure quieter members get a chance to share, sometimes disrupting the agenda flow.
case_B: A student council president runs meetings efficiently but discourages dissent and ignores quiet members.
notes: Efficient but exclusionary leadership vs disruptive but inclusive participation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A family member always remembers to send a card for every occasion but never asks how anyone is actually doing.
case_B: A family member forgets cards and dates but calls out of the blue when they sense something might be wrong, even if the timing is inconvenient.
notes: Reliable formality vs unreliable but genuine attentiveness [A/B swapped]

case_A: A community member runs a slightly chaotic open forum where everyone gets to speak, even if some comments go off-topic.
case_B: A community organizer runs a perfectly executed town hall but controls the microphone so only aligned speakers are heard.
notes: Controlled event vs chaotic but open event [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague writes a beautiful farewell message when a coworker leaves, though they never once helped that person during their time together.
case_B: A colleague says a brief, slightly abrupt goodbye to the departing coworker but had consistently helped them with day-to-day work over the past year.
notes: Eloquent farewell without substance vs abrupt goodbye after real help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person who volunteers locally every weekend shares nothing online about it but talks about it when asked, sometimes going on too long.
case_B: A person posts a long, carefully curated photo essay about their volunteer trip abroad on social media.
notes: Curated performative volunteerism vs quiet consistent volunteering [A/B swapped]

case_A: A spouse plans a romantic anniversary dinner at a fancy restaurant and spends the evening checking work emails.
case_B: A spouse forgets the exact anniversary date but cooks a slightly burned dinner the next day with genuine effort to make up for it.
notes: Fancy but distracted vs imperfect but present [A/B swapped]

case_A: A group project member produces rough slides but actively discusses content decisions with the team, changing their work based on feedback.
case_B: A group project member produces polished slides but never responds to teammates' questions about content decisions.
notes: Polished but unresponsive vs rough but collaborative [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person always says the right comforting thing at funerals but never visits the bereaved afterward.
case_B: A person is visibly uncomfortable at funerals and says little, but shows up the next week with a meal and stays to help with chores.
notes: Polished condolence without follow-through vs uncomfortable but present after [A/B swapped]

case_A: A manager quietly adjusts schedules so the team gets a lighter week after a stressful project, without making a public announcement.
case_B: A manager publicly announces a generous bonus for the team, but has privately arranged for the budget to come from canceling a training program the team wanted.
notes: Prosocial act (bonus) with hidden cost vs quiet genuine care (schedule relief) [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person makes a large donation to a school and insists on a plaque with their name in the main hallway.
case_B: A person quietly pays for a student's lunch account for the whole year after learning the family is struggling.
notes: Flashy donation for recognition vs quiet support [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague gives a peer review with honest praise and a few candid suggestions, knowing the teammate can handle constructive feedback.
case_B: A colleague writes a glowing peer review for a teammate, mainly so the teammate will reciprocate and write a positive review back.
notes: Strategic praise-trading vs honest feedback [A/B swapped]

case_A: A community leader organizes a high-profile neighborhood cleanup, using the event mainly to campaign for a local office they plan to run for.
case_B: A community member spends a Saturday picking up trash in the park alone, bringing their own bags and not telling anyone.
notes: Event as campaign tool vs quiet civic care [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent plans a simple birthday celebration at home, focusing on the games and treats the child actually asked for.
case_B: A parent throws an elaborate birthday party for their child, choosing every detail to outdo the parties other parents have thrown recently.
notes: Competitive party-planning vs child-centered celebration [A/B swapped]

case_A: An employee mentors a new hire mainly because the department head said mentoring counts toward promotion criteria.
case_B: An employee informally checks in on a new hire during their first weeks, remembering how disorienting the onboarding was when they started.
notes: Career-driven mentoring vs empathetic informal support [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person quietly sends money to a friend's medical fund through the anonymous option.
case_B: A person organizes a fundraiser for a friend's medical bills but negotiates a public thank-you post and expects future social capital.
notes: Fundraiser for social return vs anonymous help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher gives extra help to a student whose parents are on the PTA and influential in the school, spending less time with other students who need it more.
case_B: A teacher gives extra help to the student who is most behind, adjusting their schedule to make room even though the student's family has no connections.
notes: Strategic favoritism vs need-based attention [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person speaks briefly at a memorial service, sharing a simple memory that clearly mattered to them personally.
case_B: A person delivers an eloquent speech at a memorial service, having spent most of the preparation time crafting impressive rhetoric rather than reflecting on the deceased.
notes: Performance-oriented eulogy vs sincere tribute [A/B swapped]

case_A: A business owner sponsors a local sports team and makes sure the company logo is on every jersey, banner, and social media post.
case_B: A business owner sponsors a local sports team and tells the coach to just use the money however the team needs it.
notes: Branding-heavy sponsorship vs no-strings support [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague stays late to fix a production issue and just logs the fix in the normal incident tracker.
case_B: A colleague stays late to fix a production issue and then emails the entire leadership chain about it at midnight.
notes: Broadcasting heroics vs normal documentation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person hosts a lavish dinner for friends, choosing the most expensive restaurant primarily to signal their financial success.
case_B: A person invites friends over for a home-cooked meal, spending the afternoon preparing dishes they know each friend likes.
notes: Wealth display dinner vs thoughtful home-cooked meal [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent can only make half the games due to work but is fully present at each one, cheering and paying close attention.
case_B: A parent attends every single one of their child's games but spends most of the time on their phone working, rarely watching the actual play.
notes: Physical presence without engagement vs limited but fully present attendance [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student organizes a campus mental health awareness event mainly to build their resume for grad school applications.
case_B: A student quietly checks in on classmates who seem stressed during finals week, offering to grab coffee or share notes.
notes: Resume-building event vs genuine peer support [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor leaves extra vegetables from their garden on a table by the sidewalk with a help-yourself sign.
case_B: A neighbor installs a little free library on their lawn and shares photos of it weekly on the neighborhood social media group.
notes: Social media sharing project vs quiet food sharing [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team lead creates a recognition wall in the office and subtly places themselves in the most visible spot each month.
case_B: A team lead makes a habit of thanking individual contributors in private messages, noting specific things they did well.
notes: Self-serving recognition system vs private specific praise [A/B swapped]

case_A: A relative sends a handwritten card and occasionally calls to check in, even though they cannot afford expensive gifts.
case_B: A relative sends expensive gifts for every occasion but never calls, writes a personal note, or visits.
notes: Expensive but impersonal gifts vs modest but personal connection [A/B swapped]

case_A: A group member volunteers to edit the final report and rewrites everyone's sections in their own voice, taking effective authorship.
case_B: A group member volunteers to edit the final report and carefully preserves each person's contributions, only fixing errors and improving flow.
notes: Editorial takeover vs respectful editing [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person offers to walk a sick neighbor's dog and just walks the dog, returning the leash to the doorknob each time.
case_B: A person offers to walk a sick neighbor's dog and uses the daily walk as an opportunity to scope out the neighbor's property.
notes: Help-as-reconnaissance vs simple help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend plans a surprise birthday party for another friend but fills the guest list with their own contacts rather than the birthday person's actual close friends.
case_B: A friend does not throw a party but takes the birthday person to their favorite low-key spot for a quiet dinner they would actually enjoy.
notes: Self-serving party vs personalized celebration [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague occasionally brings in snacks for the team, rotating between different treats based on what people mentioned they liked.
case_B: A colleague always brings baked goods to the office on Mondays, but strategically distributes them to people who can influence their next performance review.
notes: Strategic baked goods vs genuine thoughtful snacks [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person volunteers at a food bank every Thanksgiving and always makes sure someone photographs them serving meals.
case_B: A person helps sort donations at a food bank on a random Tuesday when they have a free afternoon, without telling anyone.
notes: Photographed holiday volunteering vs unpublicized random help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student brings their own reusable containers to every meal and cleans up after others without any formal recognition.
case_B: A student leads a campus recycling initiative mainly because it qualifies them for a scholarship they want.
notes: Scholarship-motivated initiative vs unrecognized consistent practice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent reads bedtime stories to their child every night, but uses the time mainly to feel they have checked the parenting box rather than connecting with the child.
case_B: A parent reads bedtime stories to their child a few nights a week, engaging with the story and responding to the child's questions and reactions.
notes: Routine compliance vs engaged connection (even if less frequent) [A/B swapped]

case_A: A customer service representative quietly escalates a complaint through internal channels and follows up until it is resolved, without any public visibility.
case_B: A company CEO personally responds to a customer complaint on social media, knowing the public response will look good, while the underlying issue goes unfixed internally.
notes: PR-driven response vs genuine resolution [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend helps another move apartments and documents the whole thing on social media with posts about what a good friend they are.
case_B: A friend helps another move apartments, shows up on time, works steadily, and goes home without posting about it.
notes: Documented helpfulness vs quiet follow-through [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person attends a neighborhood watch meeting and mostly listens, occasionally sharing information about local safety resources.
case_B: A person organizes a neighborhood watch meeting but uses the platform to position themselves as the block authority and settle personal scores.
notes: Power-seeking organizer vs constructive participant [A/B swapped]

case_A: An employee creates a detailed onboarding document for new hires, but fills it with subtle references to their own achievements.
case_B: An employee informally walks a new hire through the first few days, answering questions and introducing them to the team.
notes: Self-promoting documentation vs personal onboarding [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent offers to help with their adult child's wedding and asks what kind of help would be most useful, then follows the couple's lead.
case_B: A parent insists on paying for their adult child's wedding and then uses that financial leverage to control decisions about the guest list and venue.
notes: Financial control vs supportive help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher creates an award ceremony for students but selects winners in a way that makes their own teaching look effective rather than based on actual student merit.
case_B: A teacher notices individual students' progress and writes brief personal notes in their folders, acknowledging specific improvements.
notes: Self-serving awards vs genuine individual recognition [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor shares extra seedlings with anyone who walks by during planting season, offering tips without gatekeeping.
case_B: A neighbor builds a beautiful community garden on their plot and then sets restrictive rules that give them control over who participates.
notes: Garden as control mechanism vs open sharing [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person sends a long, supportive text after a friend's breakup, but mainly because they have romantic interest in the friend and see an opening.
case_B: A person checks on a friend after a breakup with a short message offering to talk whenever they need to.
notes: Opportunistic comfort vs straightforward support [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague sets up a shared project tracker with open access so anyone can update their own tasks.
case_B: A colleague creates a shared project tracker and positions themselves as the gatekeeper of all updates and status changes.
notes: Tracker as control vs tracker as tool [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person donates old clothes to a shelter and insists on having the shelter tag them in a social media post.
case_B: A person drops off old clothes at a donation bin on their way to work without posting about it.
notes: Publicized donation vs quiet donation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A family member hosts the grandchildren for a weekend and focuses on making it fun, staying neutral about family dynamics.
case_B: A family member always hosts the grandchildren for summer but uses the visits to subtly turn the kids against their other parent.
notes: Grandparent alienation vs neutral caregiving [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student leads a group volunteering trip abroad and spends most of the time curating content for their personal brand rather than doing the actual service work.
case_B: A student participates in a local community service project on weekends, doing the work without any particular documentation.
notes: Voluntourism for branding vs local quiet service [A/B swapped]

case_A: A manager notices a team member seems burned out and quietly reduces their load for a week while checking in on them.
case_B: A manager creates a wellness committee at work and uses it mainly to appear progressive to upper leadership.
notes: Performative wellness vs genuine individual care [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person writes a glowing public tribute to their late mentor on social media, never having visited or called the mentor in their final months.
case_B: A person who visited their mentor regularly during their illness says little publicly, simply attending the memorial quietly.
notes: Public tribute without private care vs quiet presence [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend organizes a group gift for another friend's wedding, returning the extra money to contributors after purchasing the gift.
case_B: A friend organizes a group gift for another friend's wedding, collecting more than needed and keeping the surplus without telling anyone.
notes: Gift fund skimming vs honest handling [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor sets up a free tutoring table at the community center and uses it mainly to recruit clients for their paid tutoring business.
case_B: A neighbor spends an hour each Saturday helping kids with homework at the community center library without any business motive.
notes: Free tutoring as lead generation vs genuine free help [A/B swapped]

case_A: A manager promotes a skilled employee because they earned it, despite knowing the employee may eventually leave for a better opportunity.
case_B: A manager promotes a skilled employee partly because they deserve it and partly because the manager wants the employee to feel indebted.
notes: Mixed: deserved promotion + debt creation vs deserved promotion + accepting risk [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person helps their aging parent move into assisted living partly out of love and partly because they want access to the parent's house.
case_B: A person helps their aging parent move into assisted living out of love, even though it means they themselves have to drive much farther for visits.
notes: Mixed: love + property interest vs love + personal sacrifice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher gives a student extra attention because they see the student struggling, even though the student's parent has been critical of the school.
case_B: A teacher gives a student extra attention partly because they care and partly because the student's parent is on the school board.
notes: Mixed: care + political advantage vs care despite political friction [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague mentors a junior employee partly to help and partly to build a faction loyal to them within the department.
case_B: A colleague mentors a junior employee to help them grow, even though the junior's success might overshadow the mentor's own standing.
notes: Mixed: mentoring + faction-building vs mentoring + risking own status [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend offers emotional support during a crisis even though they are dealing with their own problems and have nothing to gain from the effort.
case_B: A friend offers emotional support during a crisis partly because they care and partly because they want to be seen as the reliable one in the group.
notes: Mixed: care + reputation vs care + personal cost [A/B swapped]

case_A: A community member organizes a winter coat drive partly to help families and partly to raise their profile before a neighborhood election.
case_B: A community member organizes a winter coat drive to help families, knowing they will have to spend their own money to cover the gaps.
notes: Mixed: charity + political positioning vs charity + personal expense [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent pushes their child to practice piano because the child asked to learn and the parent wants to support that commitment, even though the practice schedule is hard to maintain.
case_B: A parent pushes their child to practice piano partly because the child enjoys it and partly because the parent wants to brag about the child's recital performances.
notes: Mixed: supporting child + bragging vs supporting child's choice + sacrifice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student helps organize a campus fundraiser partly to help the cause and partly to list it on their graduate school application.
case_B: A student helps organize a campus fundraiser to help the cause, putting in more hours than they can comfortably spare during exam season.
notes: Mixed: cause + resume-building vs cause + personal cost [A/B swapped]

case_A: A volunteer drives elderly neighbors to appointments out of kindness, absorbing the gas cost because the neighbors are on a fixed income.
case_B: A volunteer drives elderly neighbors to appointments partly out of kindness and partly because they charge for gas at a rate that turns it into a side income.
notes: Mixed: kindness + profit-taking vs kindness + absorbing cost [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team lead advocates for a team member's raise partly because it is deserved and partly because the raise locks the person into the team when the lead needs them most.
case_B: A team lead advocates for a team member's raise because it is deserved, even though the raise makes it easier for the person to be recruited away.
notes: Mixed: advocacy + retention strategy vs advocacy + risking departure [A/B swapped]

case_A: A sibling agrees to co-sign a loan for their brother to help, understanding the risk and trusting the brother's plan even though it makes them personally vulnerable.
case_B: A sibling agrees to co-sign a loan for their brother partly to help and partly to hold financial power over him.
notes: Mixed: helping + leverage vs helping + personal risk [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor helps a new family move in partly to be welcoming and partly to assess what the family owns.
case_B: A neighbor helps a new family move in to be welcoming, adjusting their own weekend plans without any expectation of return.
notes: Mixed: welcoming + surveillance vs welcoming + sacrifice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague shares a professional contact with a coworker to help, even though the introduction might not benefit them at all.
case_B: A colleague shares a professional contact with a coworker partly to help and partly because they expect referral favors in return.
notes: Mixed: helping + expecting favors vs helping + no strings [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person donates to a friend's crowdfunding campaign partly from compassion and partly to make the friend feel obligated to support their own campaign later.
case_B: A person donates to a friend's crowdfunding campaign from compassion, not expecting anything back, even though they are tight on money themselves.
notes: Mixed: compassion + creating obligation vs compassion + personal cost [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent coaches their kid's science fair project to support the child, staying up late even though the topic is outside their expertise.
case_B: A parent coaches their kid's science fair project partly to support the child and partly to relive their own academic glory days.
notes: Mixed: support + self-gratification vs support + stretching for the child [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student leads a group project partly to learn and partly to control the outcome so they get the highest grade.
case_B: A student leads a group project to keep things on track, accepting that their own section will be weaker because they spent time helping others.
notes: Mixed: learning + grade control vs leadership + self-sacrifice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend hosts a dinner party for community, cooking everything themselves even though their kitchen is small and the process is stressful.
case_B: A friend hosts a dinner party partly for community and partly to show off their new kitchen renovation.
notes: Mixed: community + showing off vs community + effort [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher writes a strong college recommendation partly because the student deserves it and partly because successful alumni reflect well on the teacher.
case_B: A teacher writes a strong college recommendation because the student deserves it, taking the time to personalize each letter even though the workload is heavy.
notes: Mixed: deserved + reflected glory vs deserved + extra effort [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person checks on a sick coworker from genuine concern, quietly covering some of the coworker's tasks so they can rest without falling behind.
case_B: A person checks on a sick coworker partly from genuine concern and partly because being known as caring improves their reputation at work.
notes: Mixed: concern + reputation vs concern + quiet practical help [A/B swapped]
